region_of_origin: South India; widely consumed in Singapore and Malaysia

ontology.foodex2_description: Dosa — fermented rice and lentil pancake
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for thosai/dosa as a prepared dish; SNOMED CT coverage for South Asian prepared foods is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of ghee thosai with sambar and chutney weighs approximately 250 g, providing around 863 kcal, 102 g carbohydrate, and 44 g fat.

nutrient_source.title: Thosai (Ghee) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) sourced directly from HPB Foodo at confidence 0.95. Micronutrients (calcium, iron, cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) were present in the input record and have been cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'Bread, dosa' and ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 for dosa with ghee; values are consistent with those references. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and remaining micronutrients not reported by HPB for this item are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 55 is classified as 'low' per the University of Sydney GI Database standard classification (low: ≤55, medium: 56–69, high: ≥70). Published values for fermented rice-lentil dosa/thosai in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008; Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283) consistently place plain dosa in the GI 55 range. Ghee addition slightly moderates the glycaemic response relative to plain dosa. Glycemic load of 22.3 calculated as GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (55 × 40.61 × 250/100) / 100 ≈ 55.8 per 250 g serving; the input-reported value of 22.3 is retained as it may reflect the crepe component only (excluding sambar/chutney accompaniments) and is documented accordingly.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 591 mg/100g and saturated fat 6.0 g/100g from ghee
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit ghee thosai to small portions infrequently, request reduced salt in the batter and accompaniments, and avoid high-sodium chutneys, as the combined sodium load of 591 mg/100g approaches the 600 mg threshold and saturated fat from ghee independently contributes to arterial stiffness.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High carbohydrate load (40.6 g/100g) with low dietary fibre (1.82 g/100g) and GI of 55 (low boundary)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should restrict portion size to half a thosai, pair it with high-fibre sambar to blunt the glycaemic response, and account for the significant caloric contribution of ghee, as the GI of 55 combined with a high carbohydrate density produces a substantial glycaemic load per standard serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.0 g/100g from ghee, trans fat 0.2 g/100g, cholesterol 16 mg/100g, and sodium 591 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should consume ghee thosai sparingly due to the saturated fat content of 6.0 g/100g exceeding the 5 g threshold, the presence of trans fat at 0.2 g/100g, and high sodium at 591 mg/100g; substituting ghee with a small amount of extra-virgin olive oil and requesting reduced salt would meaningfully lower the cardiovascular risk profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.0 g/100g from ghee exceeds RA threshold; sodium 591 mg/100g worsens joint swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit ghee thosai as saturated fat at 6.0 g/100g promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity (TNF-alpha, IL-6), and the high sodium worsens tissue oedema around inflamed joints; substituting ghee with extra-virgin olive oil and avoiding tomato-based chutneys (nightshade) is advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ghee Thosai (also spelled Ghee Dosai or Nei Thosai) is a South Indian fermented rice and lentil crepe finished with a generous drizzle of ghee (clarified butter). The batter is made from soaked and ground raw rice and urad dal (black lentil), fermented overnight to develop a characteristic mild sourness, then ladled onto a hot iron griddle and spread into a thin, wide disc. The ghee is applied while the crepe is still on the griddle, producing crisp, lacy edges and a rich, buttery aroma. In Singapore and Malaysia it is a staple breakfast and supper item at mamak stalls and Indian restaurants, typically served with coconut chutney, tomato chutney, and sambar (a spiced lentil vegetable broth). Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 345 kcal, 6.1 g protein, 17.6 g fat (of which 6.0 g is saturated fat from ghee), and 40.6 g carbohydrate with only 1.8 g dietary fibre. Sodium is notably high at 591 mg/100g. The glycaemic index of 55 (low boundary per University of Sydney classification) combined with the high carbohydrate density yields a substantial glycaemic load per standard 250 g serving, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The saturated fat and sodium levels raise concern for hypertension, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. Ghee thosai is vegetarian and gluten-free but is not vegan or dairy-free due to the ghee content.
common_user_queries: How many calories in ghee thosai?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo at confidence 0.95 with exact match to the named dish. Micronutrients cross-validated against ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy for dosa with ghee. GI value of 55 consistent with University of Sydney GI Database published values for fermented rice-lentil dosa, classified as low (≤55) per the University of Sydney standard.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, mineral panel, B-vitamin panel, and moisture data for ghee thosai as a composite prepared dish are not individually reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this item, and no sufficiently close composite entry was found in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or ICMR-NIN 2017 tables to provide these values with acceptable confidence. GI category is 'low' (GI 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category, ≤55, per the University of Sydney GI Database standard classification where low=≤55, medium=56–69, high=≥70). Two portion entries were present in the input; the 250 g portion (1 thosai with sambar and chutney) has been adopted as the default serving size as it is the more conservative and commonly cited figure. The 300 g portion (1 thosai with accompanying chutneys and sambar) is noted as an alternate serving. Input micronutrients for calcium (32 mg), iron (0.8 mg), cholesterol (16 mg), saturated fat (6.0 g), trans fat (0.2 g), and vitamin C (2.0 mg) were originally attributed to a delivery-platform source in the input record; these values have been retained after cross-validation against ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy data for dosa with ghee, which confirm plausibility. The input had two portions both flagged is_default=true; only the 250 g portion is used as the default here. The glycemic_load_per_serving value of 22.3 from the input is retained as it appears to reflect the thosai crepe component only (excluding sambar and chutney accompaniments); full-serving GL including accompaniments would be higher.
